questions.question: Why is 'No' described as a complete sentence?

options.text: Because it is the shortest possible response
Because you are not required to provide a long list of reasons to justify your boundary
Because it is rude to say anything more
Because explanations make boundaries weaker

questions.explanation: 'No' is a complete sentence because you are not required to provide a long list of reasons why you cannot do something. If a simple 'no' feels too difficult, you can soften it with a polite buffer.

questions.question: Where should you start practicing boundaries according to this lesson?

options.text: With the most difficult person in your life
In low-stakes situations like saying 'no' to an extra pizza topping or a telemarketer
Only in therapy sessions
By setting boundaries with everyone simultaneously

questions.explanation: Start with small, low-stakes situations. Say 'no' to an extra topping, tell a telemarketer 'I'm not interested.' Each small act builds the muscle and makes it easier in more important situations.

questions.question: What physical and emotional responses should you expect when first setting boundaries?

options.text: Immediate relief and confidence
Your voice might shake and you might feel a wave of guilt -- this is normal and temporary
Nothing -- it should feel natural immediately
Anger at the other person

questions.explanation: It will feel uncomfortable. Your voice might shake, and you might feel guilt afterward. This is normal and temporary -- it is the short-term discomfort required for long-term peace.

questions.question: Which is an appropriate script for protecting your time?

options.text: 'You always waste my time with these requests'
'Thank you so much for thinking of me, but I can't take that on right now'
'I'm too busy and important for that'
'Maybe, I'll think about it' (when you mean no)

questions.explanation: 'Thank you so much for thinking of me, but I can't take that on right now' is a clear, kind, and firm boundary statement that protects your time without blaming or attacking the other person.

questions.question: What does the self-assessment at the end of the section help you do?

options.text: Get a grade on your performance
Pause and reflect honestly on where you are, identifying your next small step for growth
Compare yourself to other students
Determine if you need medication

questions.explanation: The self-assessment is not a test -- it is a mirror. It helps you pause and honestly reflect on your confidence levels across social skills and boundaries, identifying one area to focus on and one achievable next step.

title: Your Boundary Starter Pack and Self-Assessment - Quiz
